text.title: benefits-realization Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for benefits-realization as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: PM owns benefits after go-live instead of business stakeholder.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: PMs move to the next project. Benefits that outlast the PM's tenure need a business owner who is accountable for adoption and process change — the two primary enablers of realization.

failure-mode.description: Benefits register ignored after project closes ("fire and forget").
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Most benefits realize 6-18 months post-launch. A register reviewed only at go-live misses the window where corrective action (change management, re-training) can still save the benefit.

failure-mode.description: Benefits overstated in the business case to secure project approval.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Inflated benefits create a credibility trap: if realized benefits are 40% of promised, the next project from the same team faces funding skepticism regardless of merit.

failure-mode.description: Double-counting the same revenue lift across two projects in the same portfolio.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Each claimed benefit inflates the portfolio ROI. Cap total claimable benefit at portfolio level and require attribution sign-off from the CFO or PMO for any benefit over a materiality threshold.
